title: Lesson 001 – Talking About Uncertainty
grammarFocus: penso che / credo che / mi sembra che / pare che / ho l'impressione che + present subjunctive
objective: Learn to express uncertainty, impressions, opinions, and doubt using natural Italian.

sentences.italian: Penso che sia una buona idea.
sentences.literal: I think that it be a good idea.
sentences.natural: I think it's a good idea.
sentences.note: After 'penso che', Italian often uses the subjunctive: 'sia', not 'è'.

sentences.italian: Non credo che sia così semplice.
sentences.literal: I don't believe that it be so simple.
sentences.natural: I don't think it's that simple.
sentences.note: Negative opinion phrases often trigger the subjunctive.

sentences.italian: Mi sembra che tu abbia ragione.
sentences.literal: It seems to me that you have-subjunctive reason.
sentences.natural: It seems to me that you're right.
sentences.note: 'Tu abbia' is subjunctive. Italian says 'you have reason' for 'you're right'.

sentences.italian: Ho l'impressione che stiano aspettando noi.
sentences.literal: I have the impression that they be waiting for us.
sentences.natural: I get the impression they're waiting for us.

sentences.italian: Pare che il treno sia in ritardo.
sentences.literal: It seems that the train be late.
sentences.note: 'Pare che' often introduces information that feels uncertain or reported.

sentences.literal: I doubt that it work really.

sentences.italian: È possibile che abbiano già deciso.
sentences.literal: It is possible that they have-subjunctive already decided.
sentences.note: 'È possibile che' takes the subjunctive because the idea is uncertain.

sentences.italian: Non sono sicuro che venga anche lui.
sentences.literal: I am not sure that he come also him.
sentences.natural: I'm not sure he's coming too.

sentences.italian: Mi sa che abbiamo sbagliato strada.
sentences.literal: To me it knows that we have taken the wrong road.
sentences.natural: I think we've gone the wrong way.
sentences.note: 'Mi sa che' is a very common spoken phrase. It often uses the indicative.

sentences.italian: Può darsi che abbiano capito male.
sentences.literal: It can give itself that they have-subjunctive understood badly.
sentences.note: 'Può darsi che' is a very useful uncertainty phrase and takes the subjunctive.

patternDrill.literal: I think that it be better to wait.
patternDrill.natural: I think it's better to wait.

patternDrill.literal: I believe that it have sense.
It seems to me that something be missing.
patternDrill.natural: It seems like something is missing.

patternDrill.literal: It seems that they have-subjunctive changed idea.
I don't believe that it work well.
patternDrill.natural: I don't think it works well.

activeRecall.prompt: I think it's a good idea.
activeRecall.answer: Penso che sia una buona idea.

activeRecall.prompt: I don't think it's that simple.
activeRecall.answer: Non credo che sia così semplice.

activeRecall.prompt: It seems to me that you're right.
activeRecall.answer: Mi sembra che tu abbia ragione.
Pare che il treno sia in ritardo.
Può darsi che abbiano capito male.

conversationBuilder.speaker: A
conversationBuilder.italian: Pensi che sia una buona idea?
conversationBuilder.natural: Do you think it's a good idea?

conversationBuilder.speaker: B
conversationBuilder.italian: Non lo so. Non credo che sia così semplice.
conversationBuilder.natural: I don't know. I don't think it's that simple.

conversationBuilder.speaker: A
conversationBuilder.italian: Mi sembra che tu abbia qualche dubbio.
conversationBuilder.natural: It seems to me that you have some doubts.

conversationBuilder.speaker: B
conversationBuilder.italian: Sì, ho l'impressione che manchi qualcosa.
conversationBuilder.natural: Yes, I get the impression something is missing.

conversationBuilder.speaker: A
conversationBuilder.italian: Può darsi che tu abbia ragione.

grammarExplanation.intro: In Italian, when you express uncertainty, opinion, doubt, possibility, or impression, you often use the subjunctive after "che".

comparison.label: Subjunctive
comparison.italian: Penso che sia una buona idea.
comparison.english: I think it is a good idea.

grammarExplanation.explanation: The idea is no longer a plain fact; it's filtered through opinion, doubt, impression, or uncertainty.
grammarExplanation.note: Native speakers sometimes use the indicative in casual speech (especially after penso che / credo che), but the subjunctive is safer, more educated, and very useful at B1/B2.

masteryChecklist: Recognise the subjunctive forms sia, abbia, stiano, venga, funzioni.
Translate the 5 active-recall prompts into Italian.
Create 3 new sentences using penso che, mi sembra che, può darsi che.
Explain why Italian uses the subjunctive after uncertainty phrases.
